title: Is Shock Treatment Not Necessary?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: this recovered chapter retains its frozen source-established boundary and title; clear OCR character, numeral and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Greetings.

At every election, I have made it my customary democratic duty to come in person, meet voters through campaign meetings and seek support for DMK and alliance candidates. For the Srirangam by-election tomorrow, 13 February 2015, I appeal through this statement to the esteemed voters to cast their votes for DMK candidate younger brother N. Anand under the Rising Sun symbol.

The person who contested as the AIADMK candidate in Srirangam last time, received your precious votes and became Chief Minister is now seeking votes through a statement. May I not seek your votes through a statement too?

Why has this by-election been forced upon Srirangam? Ordinarily, a by-election is held when an elected Assembly member dies. This is not such an election. Jayalalithaa, whom you elected with a large majority, accumulated assets worth Rs 66 crore beyond her known income while serving as Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996. After the case was deliberately prolonged for years, the Bengaluru Special Court finally sentenced her on 27 September 2014 to four years' imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs 100 crore. Jayalalithaa lost the Chief Minister's office, her Assembly seat became vacant, and this by-election is being held to fill it.

This is not the first time Jayalalithaa has assumed office and been sent home after a court convicted her during her term. Earlier, after a conviction, she unlawfully sought election from four constituencies and her nomination papers were rejected. When her party nevertheless won a majority, she secured the Governor's cooperation and hurriedly took office despite being constitutionally disqualified. The Supreme Court of India later ruled that appointment invalid and removed her.

Now, because of one person's wrongdoing and punishment, the people of Srirangam, the political parties contesting there and their members must undergo needless hardship. The Government too incurs needless expense. I therefore appeal to you to vote for the DMK candidate at least to teach a fitting lesson to the party and leadership responsible for bringing this by-election upon the constituency.

In her appeal concerning the by-election, Jayalalithaa says it was “caused by the judgment in a case filed by conspirators.” In Jayalalithaa's dictionary, those who filed a case over her accumulation of assets and the judge who examined it at length and delivered judgment are “conspirators”! What, then, should one call the person convicted by the court in that case? A noble soul? A saint? If someone challenges wrongdoing and a court imposes punishment proportionate to the offence, do they all become conspirators?

Jayalalithaa also says in her appeal that voters will gain no benefit by supporting the DMK or other opposition candidates. True! Perhaps she is hinting that supporting the AIADMK candidate will bring Rs 2,000 per vote?

To warn and teach the ruling party a lesson, the DMK again fielded the candidate who had previously contested against Jayalalithaa. To prevent opposition votes from splitting, I was the first to issue a statement requesting every opposition party to support the DMK candidate. Yet, for reasons of their own, the BJP and CPI(M) have fielded separate candidates.

The work performed by DMK comrades in Srirangam is no ordinary effort. Frontline leaders and cadres have travelled there at their own expense and stayed without caring whether they have food or proper accommodation. “Without sleeping, without heeding hunger, without regarding bodily hardship,” they move in every direction throughout the constituency and work for the election. Reports of this continually reach and delight me.

I also learned that district leaders have kicked aside internal party issues and joined the campaign. I rejoiced: “Are they not all my Udanpirappus?” Above all, the labour of campaign committee chair younger brother Nehru cannot be described in mere words. Officially N. Anand is our candidate, but it is no exaggeration to say that Nehru has turned himself into an even more tireless candidate, spinning energetically like a top.

If Nehru has become the candidate, Treasurer younger brother M.K. Stalin has, from the beginning, become the campaign committee chair, devoting all his time and thought to the election. The General Secretary, the Professor, met me in person and set out enthusiastically, saying that he too would visit Srirangam and address meetings.

Since the by-election was announced, you have personally witnessed the ruling party's continuing anti-democratic, unlawful and disorderly acts. The DMK notified the Election Commission in time about bogus voters. I too spoke at length about it. When the Commission failed to act, the DMK approached the High Court last week.

Chief Justice Kaul and Justice Sundaresan ordered: “The Election Commission must correct the defects in the Srirangam electoral roll and publish an accurate roll two days before the by-election.” But on 9 February 2015, the Commission asked the High Court to alter the order, claiming it could not publish a revised roll before polling. The judges refused, sternly observing, “The order was issued on the basis of the Election Commission's own assurance. How can it now be changed?” They directed the Commission to file a separate petition.

Teachers reportedly say attendance has fallen at Government schools in the constituency. A weekly magazine wrote in detail that wherever Education Minister Veeramani campaigns, schoolchildren stand holding AIADMK flags and receive “special” attention for doing so.

Another weekly reported: “Minister Senthil Balaji and MP Thambidurai, responsible for seven panchayats in the Manapparai area, campaigned only in the evenings. For ten days, the AIADMK candidate did not visit this side. When candidate Valarmathi was due on 1 February, Thambidurai appeared at 7.30 a.m. Workers from the hundred-day employment scheme had been assembled at Rs 200 per person per hour. A dance troupe was brought from Pudukkottai. Reading as though from a printed sheet, Valarmathi repeatedly melted into praise that Jayalalithaa alone was God. By 1.30 p.m. at Maravanur, chicken biryani, mutton biryani, tomato rice, lemon rice and fried fish were ready for everyone, including the candidate.”

Did the AIADMK not blatantly violate the election code by erecting a vast pavilion, issuing tokens to voters and serving biryani and other meat dishes three times a day? Did DMK Organisation Secretary younger brother R.S. Bharathi not take photographs and submit them to the Chief Electoral Officer? Did the Officer not promise action? What action was taken?

Reports say police immediately register cases when the ruling party complains against the DMK or opposition, but ignore complaints against the AIADMK. Weekly magazines have written page after page that, though the present Chief Minister has not entered the constituency, his two sons are staying there and action on every issue is taken under their direction.

Women's Wing secretary and poet Kanimozhi MP, who campaigned with the Women's Wing in Srirangam, personally met the Chief Election Commissioner in Delhi. Her DMK petition described the bogus electoral roll, the lack of action despite sixty-three complaints, and the need to remove the responsible election officials immediately and appoint new ones.

She specifically stated that Manoharan, the zonal revenue officer who had served as electoral registration officer and was appointed constituency election officer, had previously applied to contest as an AIADMK candidate. Proving her statement true, the Election Commission hurriedly transferred him yesterday. That alone shows the extent of AIADMK malpractice in this by-election.

When Jayalalithaa campaigned across Srirangam in the 2011 Assembly election, she promised a permanent solution to the century-old house-site issue. Was it fulfilled? She promised increased reservation for Muslims. Did she deliver it? She promised immediate solutions to every problem in Srirangam. Were they solved?

And not only that constituency. How many schemes did Jayalalithaa announce for all Tamil Nadu? Was there a single Assembly day on which she did not issue a Rule 110 announcement? Was any other Minister permitted to read a statement concerning that Minister's department? How many announced schemes remain unimplemented?

In her appeal to Srirangam voters, Jayalalithaa claims the minority DMK Government of 2006-11 plunged Tamil Nadu into darkness through power cuts, whereas she restored AIADMK rule and placed the State on the path of progress. What is the truth? How sharply have electricity tariffs risen? What is the Electricity Board's loss? How much did milk prices rise? Did corruption in Aavin not force even the responsible Minister from office?

Bus fares were raised on the excuse of diesel prices. Diesel has since become cheaper six times. Were bus fares reduced? How much corruption exists in TASMAC? Bribery runs rampant even in selecting drivers and conductors for transport corporations.

Which section of society has escaped harm under this Government? Is there any limit to farmers' suffering? What is the condition of workers? Are factories not closing one after another? Even today newspapers report another factory closure. Weavers have lost peace, fishermen's suffering has not diminished, and the indifference shown by fanatics in power towards students' problems is appalling. Has the Chief Minister ever respected Government employees enough to summon and speak with them even once?
Law and order has deteriorated, administration staggers, bribery and corruption spread everywhere, and the country is becoming a wilderness.

Should deliverance not come? Should darkness not vanish and light spread? The Srirangam by-election offers an opportunity to place a barrier before these cruelties. It will not by itself change the Government. But should those committing these atrocities and dancing in the intoxication of power not receive shock treatment?

Esteemed voters of Srirangam, endowed with thought and the ability to weigh matters fairly, regard this as a valuable opportunity. Set aside party differences. Teach this disorderly and arrogant Government a lesson. Cast your precious votes for dear younger brother N. Anand under the Rising Sun symbol and elect him with a large majority. I ask you again and again.
With affection,
M.K.
12 February 2015
